stem: To build a schedule, a student must choose exactly one course from each of three independent categories: there are [[a]] humanities courses, [[b]] science courses, and [[c]] arts courses available. How many different schedules consisting of one course from each category are possible?
solution: The choices are made in independent stages, so by the multiplication principle the total is \( [[a]] \cdot [[b]] \cdot [[c]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds the counts from each stage instead of multiplying them.
This multiplies only the first two stages and adds the third.
This ignores the third category entirely.
This ignores the second category entirely.
This adjusts the correct product by the size of one category for no reason.
This adjusts the correct product by the size of one category for no reason.

stem: A security code consists of [[L]] characters, each chosen independently from an alphabet of [[n]] symbols. Repetition IS allowed, so the same symbol may appear in more than one position. How many different codes are possible?
solution: Each of the [[L]] positions independently has [[n]] choices (repetition allowed), so by the multiplication principle the count is \( [[n]]^{[[L]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forbids repeated symbols, computing the permutation count instead of allowing repetition.
This multiplies the alphabet size by the code length instead of raising it to that power.
This treats the code as an unordered selection without repetition.
This swaps the base and the exponent.
This is off from the correct count by one.
This is off from the correct count by one.

stem: A coach must fill [[k]] different relay positions (1st leg, 2nd leg, ..., [[k]]th leg), choosing one runner at a time from a squad of [[n]] runners. Once a runner is assigned to a leg, that runner is no longer available for any other leg. Filling the legs is a sequence of [[k]] staged choices, each with fewer runners available than the one before. How many different ways can the coach fill all [[k]] legs?
solution: The 1st leg has [[n]] choices, the 2nd leg has [[n]]-1 (one runner already used), and so on down to the [[k]]th leg with \( [[n]]-[[k]]+1 \) choices; by the multiplication principle the total is \( [[n]]([[n]]-1)\cdots([[n]]-[[k]]+1) = \dfrac{[[n]]!}{([[n]]-[[k]])!} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This treats each leg as independently having [[n]] choices, as if a runner could run more than one leg.
This treats the legs as an unordered selection rather than a staged, ordered assignment.
This fills all [[n]] squad members into legs instead of just the [[k]] legs that exist.
This only orders the [[k]] legs among themselves without accounting for the shrinking runner pool.
This is off from the correct count by [[n]].
This is off from the correct count by [[k]].

stem: A row of [[k]] beads is to be strung using [[n]] available colors. The first bead may be any of the [[n]] colors, but each subsequent bead must be a DIFFERENT color from the bead immediately before it. How many distinct colorings of the row of [[k]] beads are possible?
solution: The first bead has [[n]] choices; each of the remaining \( [[k]]-1 \) beads has only \( [[n]]-1 \) choices (any color except the one immediately before it). By the multiplication principle the total is \( [[n]]\cdot([[n]]-1)^{[[k]]-1} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This ignores the adjacency restriction and lets every bead be any of the [[n]] colors.
This multiplies the repeated factor instead of raising it to the needed power.
This applies the restricted count of \( [[n]]-1 \) to the first bead too, instead of allowing it [[n]] choices.
This treats the whole row as if no color could ever repeat anywhere, not just between adjacent beads.
This is off from the correct count by one.
This is off from the correct count by one.
